name: Florida Dept of Health
tagline: Florida's statewide public-health agency: DOH protects the health of 23M+ residents and visitors through 67 county health departments, running disease surveillance, vital records, clinical services, and emergency preparedness on a data-heavy estate centered on the state's certified Health Management System (HMS).

snapshot.label: Buying path
snapshot.value: DMS state term contracts + agency RFPs

overview: The Florida Department of Health (DOH) is the state's lead public-health agency, headquartered in Tallahassee and operating through a county health department in all 67 Florida counties. Its mission spans epidemiology and communicable-disease control, emergency preparedness and bioterrorism response, immunizations, vital records (birth/death certificates), environmental health, school health, and chronic-disease prevention for a population of 23M-plus residents plus a large visitor base. DOH is led by the State Surgeon General (who also serves as Secretary of Health) reporting to the Governor, and employs more than 17,000 people. For a technology seller, the relevant story is data- and records-intensive: DOH operates the Health Management System (HMS), an ONC-certified electronic health record (certified 2019, built on Microsoft Azure infrastructure) used across the county health departments, plus surveillance, vital-statistics, and prescription-drug-monitoring (E-FORCSE) systems. DOH does not run its own statewide IT procurement authority — technology buying flows through the Department of Management Services (DMS) state term contracts and the state's central IT-governance body, the Florida Digital Service (which remains housed under DMS after the 2026 legislative effort to move it into a new DIGIT division died in the Senate).

budget.intro: DOH is a sizable program agency but not a central IT buyer — its technology dollars ride on DMS vehicles and specific appropriations rather than a large standalone IT line. Governor DeSantis signed the $117.6B FY2026-27 budget on June 29, 2026 (after ~$810M in line-item vetoes), sustaining the ~$4.1B health-care investment framed in the 'Floridians First' proposal and fully funding Medicaid/KidCare. The proposal earmarked funds to evaluate and replace the EHR/practice-management software used by the county health departments; confirm the final enacted line before forecasting, since health-IT modernization was not called out in the signing announcement. The prior FY2025-26 budget cut about 601 DOH positions (~4.5% of the workforce), tightening the operating envelope.

stats.label: Total FY26-27 budget (signed)
stats.note: Signed by Gov. DeSantis June 29, 2026 after ~$810M in line-item vetoes; includes the ~$4.1B health-care investment

stats.label: Health-care investment (FY26-27)
stats.note: Sustained from the 'Floridians First' proposal; covers DOH and related health investments, fully funds Medicaid/KidCare

stats.label: County-EHR modernization (proposed)
stats.note: FY26-27 proposal to evaluate/implement new EHR + practice-management software across the 67 county health departments; confirm survival in the enacted budget

stats.label: Positions cut (FY25-26)
stats.note: 451 vacant + 150 staffed positions eliminated; ~4.5% of DOH's rolls

notes: A large share of DOH funding is federal and state grants tied to specific programs (immunization, emergency preparedness, HIV), which can shift with federal public-health funding decisions and create volatility independent of state general revenue.
Statewide IT governance and most technology procurement sit outside DOH at DMS — confirm which entity owns a given system or contract before forecasting.
The FY26-27 budget is now signed; confirm which line items (notably the county-EHR modernization) survived appropriations and the ~$810M of vetoes before forecasting.

initiatives.name: County health-department EHR / HMS modernization
initiatives.summary: The FY26-27 budget proposal earmarks ~$6.6M to evaluate and implement new electronic health record and practice-management software across the 67 county health departments, an upgrade meant to integrate patient records and streamline operations statewide. DOH already runs the ONC-certified Health Management System (HMS) on Microsoft Azure; a refresh or replacement is the clearest near-term modernization vehicle and the natural anchor for infrastructure, migration, and data conversations.

initiatives.name: Disease surveillance & vital-records systems
initiatives.summary: DOH operates statewide surveillance, vital-statistics, and prescription-drug-monitoring (E-FORCSE) systems that ingest and exchange high volumes of clinical and registry data across 67 counties. Sustained demand for resilient, scalable infrastructure, faster data ingestion/analytics, and interoperability across legacy and modern platforms.

initiatives.name: Data integration & analytics
initiatives.summary: Linking patient demographics, clinical reconciliation, family-health history, and exportable health information across the county system points to ongoing data-platform and analytics needs. Any AI/advanced-analytics offering must clear data-privacy and federal health-records (HIPAA/ONC) gates given the sensitivity of the data.

initiatives.name: Cybersecurity alignment to state standards
initiatives.summary: DOH must align to the statewide Florida Cybersecurity Standards (Rule 60GG-2, F.A.C., NIST-based) administered by DMS, including access management, encryption, and incident reporting. Health-data sensitivity — underscored by the RansomHub breach of DOH's Vital Statistics system and ongoing victim notifications — keeps security posture, resilience, and asset visibility in active scope.

procurement.intro: Selling into DOH means navigating two layers: the agency's own competitive procurements and Florida's centralized IT-purchasing machinery. DOH buys mission-specific systems through its own RFP/ITN solicitations, but most technology goods and services flow through Department of Management Services (DMS) state term contracts and IT enterprise agreements, governed by the state's central IT body — the Florida Digital Service, which remains under DMS (led by State CIO Warren Sponholtz) after the 2026 SB 480 / DIGIT restructuring bill died in the Senate. Confirm Dell's position on the relevant DMS state term contracts before forecasting.

vehicles.name: DMS State Term Contracts
vehicles.detail: Pre-competed statewide vehicles (hardware, software, IT enterprise agreements, and the Digital Security Solutions contract) that DOH and other agencies buy from — the fastest path for catalog-style infrastructure and licensing. Confirm Dell's coverage is active and correctly scoped.

vehicles.name: DOH agency RFP / ITN solicitations
vehicles.detail: For complex, mission-specific systems (EHR/HMS, surveillance, data platforms) DOH runs its own competitive Invitations to Negotiate and RFPs rather than buying off the shelf — the path for the county-EHR modernization and large systems work.

vehicles.name: Florida Cybersecurity Standards (Rule 60GG-2)
vehicles.detail: DMS-administered, NIST-aligned minimum security controls (access management, encryption, vulnerability management, incident reporting) that any product or cloud/as-a-service offering touching state health data must meet — clear this early.

powerStructure.name: Dr. Joseph Ladapo
powerStructure.title: State Surgeon General & Secretary of Health
powerStructure.note: Heads DOH and reports to the Governor; Senate-confirmed in 2022, still in the role as of June 2026. Sets the agency's clinical and operational agenda — the top relationship for the account. Has been publicly floated for federal roles (e.g., CDC director) and faces recurring political calls to resign; with a gubernatorial transition due Jan 2027, confirm continuity before engaging.

powerStructure.name: Office of the Governor (Ron DeSantis)
powerStructure.title: Sets DOH budget & health priorities
powerStructure.note: The Surgeon General reports to the Governor; the 'Floridians First' budget originates here (the $117.6B FY26-27 budget was signed June 29, 2026). Budget and IT-policy direction flow top-down. DeSantis is term-limited, with a new Governor taking office in Jan 2027 — expect leadership and priority shifts.

powerStructure.name: Deputy Secretaries (Scheppke / Dawkins / Lander)
powerStructure.title: Health, Operations & County Health Systems
powerStructure.note: Dr. Kenneth Scheppke (Deputy Secretary for Health), Antonio Dawkins (Operations), and Mark Lander (County Health Systems) run DOH's operational and county-facing lines below the Surgeon General — the County Health Systems seat is the natural sponsor for the 67-county EHR/HMS modernization. Confirmed on the DOH leadership roster as of June 2026.

powerStructure.name: DOH Office of Information Technology / Agency CIO
powerStructure.title: Mission health-IT, HMS & data systems
powerStructure.note: Owns DOH's mission-specific IT, the Health Management System (HMS), surveillance, and vital-records platforms. The primary technical relationship for the EHR/HMS modernization — verify the current officeholder.

powerStructure.name: DOH Information Security / ISA
powerStructure.title: Agency information security
powerStructure.note: Runs DOH's security program and Information Security Administrator function, aligned to the statewide Florida Cybersecurity Standards (Rule 60GG-2) and HIPAA — the gate for any offering touching health data.

powerStructure.name: Warren Sponholtz — Florida State CIO (Florida Digital Service)
powerStructure.title: Statewide IT governance & procurement
powerStructure.note: Central IT authority for standards, enterprise contracts, and cybersecurity; leads the Florida Digital Service, which remains under DMS after the 2026 SB 480 / DIGIT restructuring bill died in the Senate (no move to the Governor's office occurred). Sponholtz has led statewide IT since Aug 2024 and testified before Congress in May 2026 — a stable central-IT relationship above DOH's technology buys.

news.title: Gov. DeSantis signs $117.6B FY2026-27 budget (June 29) with ~$4.1B for health care
news.impact: Budget signed after ~$810M in line-item vetoes; sustains the ~$4.1B health-care investment and fully funds Medicaid/KidCare, but health-IT modernization was not called out in the signing announcement — confirm whether the proposed ~$6.6M county-EHR modernization line survived before forecasting.

news.title: SB 480 (DIGIT restructuring) dies on calendar — Florida Digital Service stays under DMS
news.impact: The bill to move statewide IT governance into a new DIGIT division under the Governor's office died March 13, 2026; the Florida Digital Service remains at DMS under State CIO Warren Sponholtz. The central-IT relationship map above DOH's technology buys is stable — no DIGIT transition to track for now.

news.title: Florida State CIO Sponholtz testifies before Congress on state cybersecurity
news.impact: Warren Sponholtz (leading Florida IT since Aug 2024) testified before a U.S. House committee in May 2026, reinforcing the state's NIST-aligned cybersecurity posture — the standards regime (Rule 60GG-2) any DOH-facing cloud or as-a-service offering must clear remains front and center.

news.title: Governor's 'Floridians First' FY26-27 proposal earmarks county-EHR modernization
news.impact: Proposal earmarked ~$6.6M to evaluate and replace county health-department EHR/practice-management software — the clearest near-term modernization opening at DOH; now track survival in the enacted budget.

news.title: FY25-26 budget eliminates ~601 DOH positions
news.impact: 451 vacant plus 150 staffed positions cut (~4.5% of the workforce), a ~$34M general-revenue impact — tighter staffing raises the pull toward automation, managed services, and efficiency-driven modernization.

dellActions: Anchor on the county health-department EHR / HMS modernization: position ISG infrastructure, hybrid-cloud, storage, and migration services against the proposed ~$6.6M refresh and the existing Azure-based Health Management System across 67 counties.

Note the SB 480 / DIGIT restructuring died in the 2026 Legislature — the Florida Digital Service stays under DMS with State CIO Warren Sponholtz, so build to a stable central-IT layer above DOH's buys, and watch for a fresh restructuring push after the Jan 2027 gubernatorial transition.

Confirm Dell's position on DMS state term contracts and IT enterprise agreements — the primary catalog path into DOH — and watch DOH's own ITN/RFP solicitations for the larger EHR and data-platform procurements.
Lead with resilient, scalable infrastructure for surveillance, vital records, and high-volume clinical data; frame around data integration and analytics needs across the county system.

Align security and AI offerings to the Florida Cybersecurity Standards (Rule 60GG-2, NIST-based) and HIPAA/ONC requirements given health-data sensitivity — clear these gates early for any cloud or as-a-service play.
